notes.title: Atomic Mass and Composition
points: Nucleus consists of protons (positive charge) and neutrons (neutral).
Atomic Mass Unit (u): Defined as 1/12th of the mass of a Carbon-12 atom.
Isotopes: Same Z, different A (e.g., H-1, H-2, H-3).
Isobars: Same A, different Z (e.g., H-3 and He-3).
Isotones: Same number of neutrons (A-Z).

notes.title: Size of the Nucleus
points: Volume of nucleus is directly proportional to the mass number A.
Nuclear density is constant and independent of A (approx 2.3 x 10^17 kg/m^3).

notes.label: Nuclear Radius
conditions: R0 is a constant, approximately 1.2 fm (1.2 x 10^-15 m).

notes.title: Mass-Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy
points: Mass Defect (Delta M): The difference between the sum of masses of constituents and the actual mass of the nucleus.
Binding Energy (Eb): The energy required to separate the nucleus into its constituent nucleons.
Eb = (Delta M)c^2.

notes.label: Mass Defect
conditions: M is the mass of the nucleus.
mp and mn are masses of proton and neutron.

notes.title: Binding Energy per Nucleon Curve
notes.caption: Ebn is practically constant (8 MeV) for 30 < A < 170. Maximum (8.75 MeV) for Fe-56.

notes.title: Conclusions from Binding Energy Curve
points: Constancy of Ebn in the range 30 < A < 170 indicates that nuclear force is short-ranged and saturates.
Lower Ebn for light nuclei (A<30) indicates fusion is energetically favourable.
Lower Ebn for heavy nuclei (A>170) indicates fission is energetically favourable.

notes.title: Nuclear Force
points: The strong attractive force that holds nucleons together.
Short Range: Operates only up to ~3 fm.
Charge Independent: Force between p-p, n-n, and p-n is approximately the same.
Non-central and spin-dependent.
Does not depend on electric charge.

notes.title: Potential Energy of Nucleon Pair
notes.caption: Minimum PE at r0 ~ 0.8 fm. Attractive for r > r0, Repulsive for r < r0.

notes.title: Radioactivity
points: Spontaneous emission of radiation (alpha, beta, gamma) by unstable nuclei.
It is a random, statistical process.

notes.label: Law of Radioactive Decay
conditions: N is number of undecayed nuclei at time t.

notes.title: Exponential Decay Curve
notes.caption: Number of active nuclei N decreases exponentially with time.

notes.label: Half Life
conditions: Time taken for number of nuclei to reduce to half.

notes.label: Mean Life
conditions: Average time a nucleus exists before decaying.

notes.title: Alpha Decay
points: Emission of Helium nucleus (2 protons, 2 neutrons).
Mass number decreases by 4, Atomic number decreases by 2.
Q value = (Mass of parent - Mass of products)c^2.

notes.label: Alpha Decay Equation
conditions: Process occurs spontaneously only if Q > 0.

notes.title: Beta Decay
points: Beta Minus (B-): Neutron converts to proton, electron, and antineutrino.
Beta Plus (B+): Proton converts to neutron, positron, and neutrino.
Neutrino/Antineutrino accounts for conservation of energy and momentum.

notes.latex: n \to p + e^- + \bar{\nu} \quad (\beta^-) \quad ; \quad p \to n + e^+ + \nu \quad (\beta^+)
notes.label: Beta Decay Equations
conditions: In B-, Z increases by 1. In B+, Z decreases by 1. A remains constant.

notes.title: Gamma Decay
points: Emission of high energy photons when nucleus transitions from excited state to ground state.
Often follows alpha or beta decay.
No change in Z or A.

notes.title: Energy Levels (Gamma Decay)
notes.caption: Example: Co-60 beta decays to excited Ni-60, which emits gamma rays to reach ground state.

notes.title: Nuclear Fission
points: Splitting of a heavy nucleus (e.g., U-235) into two lighter nuclei of comparable masses.
Releases huge energy (approx 200 MeV per fission) and neutrons.
Neutrons released can trigger further fission (Chain Reaction).

result.direction: Moderator (Heavy water/Graphite) slows neutrons; Control rods (Cd/Boron) absorb neutrons; Coolant transfers heat.

notes.title: Nuclear Reactor Schematic
notes.caption: Core containing fuel rods, moderator, and control rods.

notes.title: Nuclear Fusion
points: Fusion of two light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus.
Releases energy per unit mass greater than fission.
Requires extremely high temperature (Thermonuclear fusion) to overcome Coulomb repulsion.

notes.title: Energy Generation in Sun (Proton-Proton Cycle)
assumptions: High Temperature (~1.5 x 10^7 K) in core.

result.direction: Net result: 4 Hydrogen nuclei fuse to form Helium.

ncert_golden_lines: The density of nuclear matter is approximately 2.3 x 10^17 kg m^-3. This density is very large compared to ordinary matter.
The nuclear force between two nucleons does not depend on the electric charge.
The binding energy per nucleon is practically constant, independent of the atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number.
Radioactivity is an indication of the instability of nuclei.
The half-life T1/2 is the time at which both N and R have been reduced to one-half their initial values.
Fusion of hydrogen nuclei into helium nuclei is the source of energy of all stars including our sun.

diagram_descriptors.detailed_description: Ebn is practically constant (8 MeV) for 30 < A < 170. Maximum (8.75 MeV) for Fe-56.
Minimum PE at r0 ~ 0.8 fm. Attractive for r > r0, Repulsive for r < r0.
Number of active nuclei N decreases exponentially with time.
Example: Co-60 beta decays to excited Ni-60, which emits gamma rays to reach ground state.
Core containing fuel rods, moderator, and control rods.
